plainMeaning: Revered teacher, please permit me to step into the small sacred space right around you.

wordByWord.gloss: grant permission, allow, give leave (to me)
wordByWord.etymology: From Sanskrit anu-√jñā 'to permit, sanction, consent'; honorific 2nd-person imperative (Skt anujānīta → Prakrit aṇujāṇaha), addressed respectfully to the guru.
wordByWord.notes: Plural/honorific imperative — a request, not a command; the disciple petitions, the guru grants.

wordByWord.etymology: Enclitic dative/genitive of the 1st-person pronoun, Skt me (mahyam); unchanged in Prakrit.
wordByWord.notes: Dative of the person on whose behalf permission is sought.

wordByWord.gloss: the avagraha — the limited, consecrated space immediately around the guru (here, leave to enter it)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt mita-avagraha → mit-avaggaha → Prakrit miuggaha; mita 'measured, limited' + avagraha 'allotted/withheld space, claimed ground'; accusative singular -aṁ as the object of an implied 'to enter'.
wordByWord.notes: Not the grammatical avagraha (elision-sign); here the ritually regulated zone around the teacher into which one may not intrude unbidden.

literalTranslation: Grant me permission [for entering] the limited consecrated space [around you].
idiomaticTranslation: O revered one, grant me leave to enter the hallowed space close to you.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having in the previous unit asked only for leave to bow, the disciple now takes the next bodily step: permission to cross into the guru's immediate ground. The whole unit is three words — an imperative, a dative, and a single accusative object — yet it encodes an entire ethic of approach. 'Aṇujāṇaha' is framed as a petition in the honorific, so the movement forward is never the disciple's to take unilaterally; it is conferred. 'Me' keeps the request personal and humble — leave for this one supplicant, here and now. The hinge is 'miuggahaṁ' (mita-avagraha): the measured, withheld space ringing the teacher. The word mita ('limited') is doctrinally precise — the disciple does not ask to occupy the guru's seat or dignity, only to draw near to a permitted, bounded distance. The accusative, with no verb of motion spoken aloud, lets the gesture itself supply 'to enter': speech and the forward step coincide. The force of the verse is the inversion of ordinary closeness — nearness to the worthy is not seized by eagerness but received by consent. Where verse 1 secured the right to honor, verse 2 secures the right to be near enough to honor, preparing the well-being inquiry that follows. Its brevity is its discipline: a single asked-and-granted threshold, crossed without presumption.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The avagraha is a regulated zone, and Jain monastic discipline treats space itself as something that may not be encroached upon without sammati (consent) — an extension of restraint (saṁyama) and non-harm into the geometry of approach. To enter unbidden would be a subtle āśātanā (slight) of the very kind the sūtra's closing unit later confesses; asking first forecloses it. The verse thus enacts vinaya (reverent humility) as conduct rather than sentiment: reverence proven by self-limitation. The qualifier mita teaches measure — devotion that knows its bounds honors the teacher more truly than devotion that overruns them.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The reciter speaks this while making the small forward movement toward the sthāpanācārya or guru — word and step as one act. Beyond ritual, it trains a daily habit: seek consent before entering another's space or attention, and keep one's claim on a respected person's nearness measured, never presumptuous.

crossReferences.text: Vandanaka (Third Āvaśyaka) — the avagraha-praveśa among the 25 āvaśyaka-sthānas

sources.title: H. D. T. Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Sanskrit Dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit gloss of aṇujāṇaha (anu-√jñā) and miuggaha as mita-avagraha, distinct from the grammatical avagraha sign

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying the tokens: anu-√jñā 'to permit', and avagraha 'allotted/withheld space'; mita 'measured, limited'

sources.title: Āvaśyaka-sūtra and Niryukti tradition (Śvetāmbara Āgama), via Jain eLibrary
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the avagraha-praveśa step within the dvādaśāvarta vandana and its place among the 25 āvaśyaka-sthānas

notes: The verse is a single ritual unit of three words, not a metrical gāthā. 'miuggahaṁ' is read as mita-avagraha (the limited consecrated space around the guru), per the tradition knowledge base; it must not be confused with the homonymous grammatical avagraha (elision mark), a false lead returned by generic web sources. The verb of motion ('to enter') is unspoken and supplied by the accompanying gesture; some readings render it 'permit me [to use/occupy] your avagraha'. Spelling 'miuggahaṁ/miuggaham' and the anusvāra are preserved as printed in the Anchalgachchha edition. Web verification returned only generic material; doctrinal claims rest on the supplied tradition knowledge base and standard Āvaśyaka scholarship and are not independently page-verified.
